design_philosophy.core_loop: Catch colored tiles from conveyor belt, arrange them into matching rows/patterns (horizontal/vertical/diagonal lines of 3+), clear matches to create cascades, manage limited paddle and well capacity
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Space management tension between catching incoming tiles and strategic placement in a 5x5 well with 5-tile paddle capacity; resource tension between available drops and well fullness
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Puzzle mastery through tile manipulation; achieving chain reactions and cascading clears; progression through escalating wave difficulty with strategic wave selection
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game is explicitly inspired by Tetris and tic-tac-toe but asserts its own identity through: (1) catch-and-place mechanic rather than rotation, (2) player-controlled tile-falling timing, (3) diagonal matching alongside orthogonal, (4) wave-based progression with difficulty choice, (5) emphasis on combo scoring over survival. Developer Dave Akers stated goal was 'produce something playable, compact and relatively quick to develop' - prioritizes accessibility and swift gameplay loops over mechanical depth

difficulty.lives_system: Drop-based system: player receives drops that deplete when tiles fall off conveyor uncaught or paddle is full; game ends when drops exhausted
difficulty.continues_system: Wave-selection system grants player agency: at game start and after every 5th wave, player chooses next wave from three options; selecting later waves awards bonus points and grants additional drops, allowing difficulty escalation with reward trade-off
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint after every 5th wave; drop meter resets between checkpoint intervals
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High player agency: player strategically selects which wave to attempt next, choosing between guaranteed progression (easy waves) versus risk/reward (harder waves with bonus drops). Player controls paddle position, tile-dropping timing, and can flip tiles up-conveyor or accelerate downward motion

progression.unlock_mechanism: 100 waves divided into 20 groups of 5 waves each; waves presented as selectable options after every 5-wave interval rather than strict linear unlock. Each subsequent wave generally increases difficulty via higher tile volumes, pattern complexity, or specific scoring/survival objectives
replayability_hooks: Wave selection creates branching progression paths; player can choose easier or harder waves affecting final score and available drops
Bonus point scoring systems reward combo plays, empty well spaces, and unconsumed conveyor tiles, incentivizing optimization
100 distinct waves with varying objectives (set number of Klaxs, point thresholds, survival counts) create content variety

sources_used: tcrf - Klax (NES) The Cutting Room Floor
